content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 4, 2003

Edward L. Knapp
Chief Assistant County Counsel
El Dorado County
330 Fair Lane
Placerville, CA 95667

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-02-229

Dear Mr. Knapp:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the grand jurors of El Dorado County regarding the conflict of interest code provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

1. Does the grand jury of El Dorado County continue to be a local government agency, which is required to adopt its own code?

2. Is the El Dorado County Board of Supervisors the code reviewing body for the grand jury?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Yes. The grand jury is a local government agency subject to the conflict of interest code provisions of the Act.

2. Yes. The board of supervisors is the code reviewing body for the conflict of interest code governing the grand jury because the grand jury is an agency with jurisdiction wholly within El Dorado County for purposes of the Act's conflict of interest code rules.

________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-02-229
Page No. 2

FACTS

The County of El Dorado recently eliminated grand jurors from the appendix of
designated employees in the county's local conflict of interest code. The county
concluded that the grand jury was a local government agency which was required to
adopt its own conflict of interest code and submit it for approval to the presiding judge of
the superior court as the code reviewing body.

Up until the late 1990s, El Dorado County courts were staffed with county
employees, and therefore certain court employees (other than judges) were designated in
El Dorado County's conflict of interest code along with other county employees. Grand
jurors were included within the group of court employees. For purposes of implementing
new trial court funding legislation, trial court employees are no longer county employees.
As a result, during its biannual update in March 2001, El Dorado County eliminated local
court employees from the county's conflict of interest code. In the process, the county
also realized that grand jurors were not county employees and therefore eliminated them
from the county's conflict of interest code at the same time.

Due to these events, the county has not previously had to address the issue of
what would be the appropriate code reviewing body for the local grand jury's conflict of
interest code. The county's conclusion that the proper code reviewing body for the grand
jury's code is the presiding judge of the superior court agrees with earlier FPPC
positions, but is in disagreement with later conclusions.

Additionally, you have submitted for our consideration a letter from Steve
Cascioppo, Court Executive Officer of the Superior Court of El Dorado County,
expressing the Court's view of the issues presented in your request. You have requested
that we take into account the view of the Court that the grand jury has the county board of
supervisors as its code reviewing body pursuant to section 82011(b).

ANALYSIS

Section 87300 requires that every agency "adopt and promulgate" a conflict of
interest code. The term "agency," as defined in section 82003 includes a "local
government agency."

Local Government Agency

"Local government agency" is defined as:

"[A] county, city or district of any kind including school
district, or any other local or regional political subdivision,
or any department, division, bureau, office, board,
commission or other agency of the foregoing." (Section
82041.)

File No. A-02-229
Page No. 3

Grand juries are local government agencies and are required to adopt a conflict of interest
code. (Bryan Advice Letter, No. A-92-284; See also Bourbeau Advice Letter, No. I-89-
127 and Whittington Advice Letter, No. A-85-185.) Therefore, the grand jury of El
Dorado County continues to be a local government agency subject to the Act's conflict of
interest code rules and must adopt a conflict of interest code if it is no longer included in
the county's code.

Specifically, the grand jury must "adopt and promulgate" a conflict of interest
code in compliance with Chapter 7, Article 3 of the Act. Such a conflict of interest code
must include the specific types of investments, business positions, interests in real
property, and sources of income which are reportable, in addition to other information
described in section 87302. However, please note that a conflict of interest code is not
effective until it has been approved by the agency's code reviewing body. (Section
87303.)²

Code Reviewing Body

Under section 82011(b), the board of supervisors is the code reviewing body with
respect to the conflict of interest code of any "local government agency," other than a city
agency with jurisdiction wholly within the county.³ (Section 82011(b).)

The issue of who is the code reviewing body for a grand jury was first examined
by the Commission in 1985. It was the Commission's initial belief that the appropriate
code reviewing body for a grand jury would be the presiding judge of the superior court.
(See Memorandum to Commissioners regarding "Attached Advice Letter to David
Whittington re Grand Juries," December 19, 1985.) In June 1986, the grand jury issue
was presented to the Commission which considered a staff memorandum stating that
grand juries were subject to the immediate supervision of the superior court in the county
where the grand jury sits. (Memorandum to Commissioners regarding "Grand Jury
Disclosure," May 28, 1986.) At that June meeting, the Commission ultimately voted to
advise county counsels that grand jurors should file disclosure statements at the
beginning and conclusion of their terms. (Commission Meeting Minutes, June 10, 1986.)
Staff was additionally directed to communicate with the counties on these matters.

In a memorandum submitted the following month, staff advised the Commission
that the board of supervisors should probably function as the code reviewing body for the
conflict of interest code governing grand jurors, based on arguments raised by Judge
Gualco of the Sacramento County Superior Court in a June 1986 letter to the
Commission. (Memorandum to Commissioners regarding "Update on Financial
Disclosure By Grand Jurors," August 1, 1986.) Among these arguments, Judge Gualco

² Additionally, the code reviewing body is required to take one of the following actions within 90
days of receiving the proposed code: a) approve the proposed code as submitted; b) revise the proposed
code and approve it as revised, or c) return the proposed code to the agency for revision and resubmission
within 60 days.
³ We do not address herein whether a grand jury may also constitute an "agency of the judicial
branch of government" for purposes of section 82011(b) since the Commission has already determined that
a grand jury is a "local government agency."

File No. A-02-229
Page No. 4

pointed out that the grand jury acts independently, that its conduct and deliberations are
to be held in confidence, that the superior court cannot participate in the grand jury
proceedings except to advise and return true bills, and that the grand jury is an almost
autonomous body over which the court has limited direction. (See Letter to
Commissioners from Eugene T. Gualco, June 9, 1986.)

The Commission agreed with staff's opinion and subsequently advised that the
court has no control, supervision, or direction on the subjects of inquiries by the grand
jury and further that the grand jury functions as an autonomous body over which the court has
limited direction. (Memorandum to Interested Parties regarding "Grand Juror Conflict of
Interest Codes," March 3, 1987, attachment entitled "Disclosure by Members of the
Grand Jury.") Since grand juries were found not to be subject to the immediate
supervision of the court, the appropriate code reviewing body was determined to be the
respective county board of supervisors. (Ibid.)

Reaffirmation that the board of supervisors is the code reviewing body for a grand
jury was issued in a 1987 letter by staff and distributed to all county counsels among
other interested persons (Letter to the Presiding Judge of the Superior Court of the
County of Amador, March 4, 1987) and again, in the Botz Advice Letter, No. A-87-083
and Keeley Advice Letter, No. I-89-090. This is the current Commission advice.

You disagree that the grand jury is an agency with jurisdiction wholly within the
county and that section 82011(b) governs the grand jury. On this issue, you point out that
the grand jury is empowered to investigate and report on any special purpose assessing or
taxing district located wholly or partly in the county, and provide the El Dorado Hills
County Water District as an example, which provides fire protection to part of
Sacramento County.

We have recently advised that, for purposes of determining an official's reporting
obligation, it is sufficient that the agency have the ability to exercise some facet of its
jurisdiction within a particular geographic area. (Chapman Advice Letter, No. A-02-
115.) With regard to the grand jury, the relevant geographic demarcation is the county
boundary. Specifically, the grand jury's ability to investigate a particular agency arises
solely from the agency's function within the county jurisdiction.

Although you have raised a number of issues as to why you believe the presiding
judge of the superior court should instead serve as the code reviewing body, the
Commission has already considered this issue and has determined that the board of
supervisors is the appropriate code reviewing body. The facts you have submitted do not
articulate a novel question nor are they sufficient to change this longstanding
Commission advice. Consequently, we conclude that the code reviewing body for the
conflict of interest code which governs the El Dorado grand jury is the El Dorado County
Board of Supervisors.⁴

⁴ Under section 87500(e), each grand juror must file one original statement of economic interest
with the grand jury or with the board of supervisors, as provided by the grand jury's conflict of interest
code, as approved by the board of supervisors as the code reviewing body.

File No. A-02-229
Page No. 5

Please note that section 87311 specifies that "[t]he review and preparation of
Conflict of Interest Codes by local government agencies shall be carried out under
procedures which guarantee to officers, employees, members and consultants of the
agency and to residents of the jurisdiction adequate notice and a fair opportunity to
present their views." Once a code is adopted, a grand juror may submit a petition
requesting an amendment (Section 87307), and if denied, may petition for judicial review
(Section 87308).

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: [signature] Natalie Bocamegra
Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosures

which is required to adopt its own code?
2. Is the El Dorado County Board of Supervisors the code reviewing body for the grand
jury?

CONCLUSION: 1. Yes. The grand jury is a local government agency subject to the conflict of interest
code provisions of the Act.
2. Yes. The board of supervisors is the code reviewing body for the conflict of interest
code goveming the grand jury because the grand jury is an agency with jurisdiction
wholly within El Dorado County forpurposes of the Act's conflict of interest code rules.
